subsystem: libgd XBM (X BitMap) image decoder — gdImageCreateFromXbm(), the loader that turns an XBM text file into an in-memory gdImage. This is the routine PHP's imagecreatefromxbm() and any libgd consumer call to decode attacker-supplied XBM files.
provenance: Identified by the API surface: gdImagePtr, gdImageCreate(), gdImageColorAllocate(), gdImageSetPixel(), gdImageDestroy(), the im->sx/im->sy image fields, and the php_gd_error() logging helper are all libgd / php-gd symbols. The function name gdImageCreateFromXbm and the XBM grammar it parses (#define <name>_width, <name>_height, 'static unsigned char <name>_bits[] = {') are the canonical libgd XBM reader. The sample banner 'primevul_c_train_3022_vuln' and the file name train_0123_cwe908.c indicate a labeled vulnerable sample.
trust_boundary: The single argument is FILE *fd — the raw bytes of an XBM file. In the PHP/libgd usage this file is fully attacker-controlled (uploaded image, remote-fetched asset, user-named path). Every field the parser extracts — the width/height #define values, the array element type keyword, and every hex byte in the bits[] initializer — is hostile input. There is NO authentication, checksum, or size sanity gate on this data before it drives allocation sizes (gdImageCreate) and pixel writes (gdImageSetPixel). The parser must therefore treat width, height, the derived byte count, and every getc() byte as adversarial.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point takes a single already-opened stdio FILE handle and returns a gdImagePtr (an allocated image) on success or 0 on any failure. There is no separate length argument, so the parser learns the image geometry entirely from the textual contents of the file: it must read #define lines for width and height and then a C array initializer of hex bytes. Because the caller passes only a FILE*, all bounds discipline lives inside this one function.

Local state. fline is the line buffer filled by fgets and iname is the sscanf %s destination for identifier tokens, both sized MAX_XBM_LINE_SIZE (an external macro not shown). value is the signed int target of the #define %d parse; width and height are unsigned ints seeded to 0 and later assigned from (unsigned int)value. max_bit encodes the array element width (128 for 8-bit char arrays, 32768 for 16-bit short arrays) and doubles as a 'have we seen a data array yet' flag. bytes is a SIGNED int that will hold the computed number of hex bytes to read. h[8] is a tiny scratch buffer that accumulates the 2 or 4 hex characters of one array element as text. b is an unsigned int that receives the parsed hex value — critically, b is declared here with NO initializer, so its value is indeterminate until something writes it.

The file is rewound to the start, then read line by line with fgets, which is bounded to MAX_XBM_LINE_SIZE and always NUL-terminates, so fline itself cannot overflow. Line 21 defensively re-terminates the last byte. Line 22 rejects any line whose length hit MAX_XBM_LINE_SIZE-1 (i.e. a line longer than the buffer, missing its trailing newline) by returning 0 — this is a good guard that prevents partially-read tokens from being mis-parsed. This header-parsing loop continues until the bits[] marker is found (break) or fgets hits EOF.

If the line matches the '#define <ident> <int>' shape, sscanf writes the identifier into iname and the number into the SIGNED int value. strrchr finds the last '_' so a name like 'foo_width' yields the suffix 'width'; if there is no underscore the whole name is used. When the suffix is 'width' or 'height' the signed value is cast to unsigned int and stored. Note there is NO range check on value: a negative or absurdly large integer is accepted verbatim, and the signed->unsigned cast turns a negative into a near-UINT_MAX value. These two numbers are the sole source of the image geometry.

For non-#define lines, the code tries to match the C array declaration that begins the bitmap data. If the element type is (unsigned) char, max_bit becomes 128 (the high bit of an 8-bit value); if it is (unsigned) short, max_bit becomes 32768 (the high bit of a 16-bit value). max_bit thus both selects how many hex text characters make up one element (2 vs 4) and provides the loop bound over bits. The %s here has no field-width limit, copying an unbounded identifier token from fline into iname.

Once an array declaration has been recognized (max_bit set), the byte count of the bitmap is computed as (width + 7) / 8 * height — bits-per-row rounded up to whole bytes, times the number of rows. If that product is zero (width or height was 0) the loader bails. The suffix of the array identifier is then checked for 'bits[]'; when found, the header phase ends via break and the file cursor is left positioned at the start of the data. All the arithmetic here is on the attacker-controlled width/height with no upper bound and no overflow check, and bytes is a signed int receiving an unsigned product.

After the header loop, the loader requires both a nonzero byte count and a set element type, otherwise returns 0. It then creates the image canvas at the attacker-supplied width x height with gdImageCreate (which internally allocates width*height bytes plus row pointers) and checks only for a NULL result. Two palette colors are allocated: index 0 = white (background), index 1 = black (set pixels). From here on im->sx == width and im->sy == height.

h[2] and h[4] are pre-set to NUL so that a 2-char (char array) element parses as a 2-digit hex string and a 4-char (short array) element parses as a 4-digit string. The outer loop runs bytes times, once per bitmap element. For each element it scans forward byte by byte looking for the 'x' of the '0x..' hex literal; if EOF is hit first it sets fail and the outer loop terminates, leading to the EOF error path. This inner scan is how it steps over commas, whitespace, and the '0' before each 'x'.

Immediately after the 'x', the code reads the two hex text characters into h[0] and h[1]. For 16-bit short arrays (max_bit == 32768) it reads two more into h[2] and h[3], overwriting the NUL that was at h[2] so the NUL at h[4] now terminates a 4-char string. EOF at any point breaks out to the incomplete-image path. Crucially the bytes copied into h are taken verbatim from the file and are NOT validated to be hex digits — h may contain arbitrary non-hex characters at this point.

The hex text in h is converted to the unsigned int b, and each bit of b (from LSB up to max_bit) becomes one pixel: black if the bit is set, white otherwise. x advances per pixel and wraps to the next row when it reaches im->sx; when y reaches im->sy the fully-populated image is returned. The bit loop is capped at max_bit so it emits at most 8 or 16 pixels per element. The row/column wrap is bounded by im->sx/im->sy so the pixel writes themselves stay in-canvas. The load-bearing weakness is the very first statement: sscanf's return value is discarded, so b is trusted whether or not the conversion actually happened.

If the outer loop finishes (or breaks on EOF) without the inner loop returning a completed image, the loader logs an error, destroys the partially-filled image, and returns 0. This is the failure epilogue reached when the file is truncated relative to the declared width*height.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: PRIMARY BUG (CWE-908, Use of Uninitialized Resource). b is declared without an initializer at line 17. Line 107 calls sscanf(h, "%x", &b) but ignores the return value. If h does not begin with a valid hexadecimal digit — which the attacker fully controls, e.g. by placing 'x' followed by non-hex bytes like 'zz' or by a whitespace/garbage sequence — sscanf performs ZERO conversions and leaves b completely untouched. b then holds whatever indeterminate stack garbage was there, and line 109 reads it in (b & bit) to decide every pixel of that element. The invariant the implementation must uphold is: never consume b unless sscanf returned 1 (or initialize b = 0 before the call). The impact is use of an uninitialized value — nondeterministic image content at minimum, and an information-disclosure / uninitialized-read defect that static analyzers and memory sanitizers flag, with the exact class the sample is labeled (CWE-908). The fix is to check sscanf(...) == 1 or initialize b.

Integer overflow (CWE-190). width and height are unsigned ints derived directly from attacker input with no upper bound (lines 33/36). (width + 7) overflows to a tiny value when width is within 7 of UINT_MAX, and (…) * height overflows the 32-bit unsigned product for large geometries; the result is then stored into the SIGNED int bytes, which can wrap to a negative or otherwise wildly wrong count. The invariant is: reject width/height beyond a sane maximum and compute the byte count in a width that cannot overflow (or check for overflow) BEFORE using it. Consequences: bytes going negative makes the for-loop body (i < bytes) never execute so the image is left blank/incomplete; a wrapped-small positive bytes desynchronizes the amount of data read from the declared canvas size; and because gdImageCreate is called first with the raw huge width/height, the overflow also propagates into libgd's own sx*sy allocation math.

Unbounded resource allocation / DoS (CWE-789, CWE-400). width and height reach here unchecked; only bytes==0 (width or height literally 0) is filtered. An attacker can declare, e.g., '#define x_width 60000' and '#define x_height 60000', making gdImageCreate attempt to allocate on the order of 3.6 GB of pixel data plus per-row pointer arrays. The NULL check only handles the case where allocation fails cleanly; the real damage is the memory-exhaustion / crash and, on 32-bit, the width*height size computation inside gdImageCreate itself overflowing (a separate out-of-bounds primitive). The invariant: clamp width and height to a documented maximum before allocation.

Signed-to-unsigned conversion of unvalidated input (CWE-20 / CWE-190 feeder). value is parsed with %d so it can be negative; (unsigned int)(-1) is 4294967295. A hostile '#define x_width -1' therefore sets width to ~4 billion, which then feeds both the byte-count arithmetic at line 49 and the allocation at line 68. The invariant is: reject value <= 0 or value > MAX_ALLOWED before casting. This is the root that arms both the integer-overflow and the resource-exhaustion findings above.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
if ( sscanf(fline, "static unsigned char %s = {", iname) == 1
			  || sscanf(fline, "static char %s = {", iname) == 1)
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Latent unbounded %s (CWE-120 / CWE-787, mitigated here but fragile). Every sscanf %s writing into iname (lines 25, 39, 40, 43, 44) has no field-width limit. In this function it happens to be safe because the source fline is fgets-bounded to MAX_XBM_LINE_SIZE and iname is the same size, so the longest possible token still fits; the line-length guard at line 22 further caps input. The latent hazard is a maintenance one: iname and fline are only coincidentally equal-sized, and %s stops at whitespace — if either buffer size ever diverged, or if the format were reused on a longer source, this is a stack buffer overflow. The invariant: bound every %s with an explicit field width (e.g. %255s).

summary: A libgd XBM decoder that parses a fully attacker-controlled file. The primary, sample-matching defect is a use-of-uninitialized-value (CWE-908): sscanf(h, "%x", &b) ignores its return value, so a non-hex byte sequence leaves the unsigned int b uninitialized before it is read at gdImageSetPixel. Secondary contract failures are attacker-controlled width/height with no range check driving an unchecked (width+7)/8*height integer-overflow computation and an unbounded gdImageCreate allocation (memory-exhaustion / size-overflow DoS).

reviewed_anchors.reason: fd is fully attacker-controlled file input with no length parameter; all geometry/data trust originates here.
Filled only via fgets bounded to MAX_XBM_LINE_SIZE and re-NUL-terminated (line 21); cannot overflow.
Destination of width-less sscanf %s; safe today only because it equals fline's size — latent overflow, flagged.
Signed %d target cast to unsigned width/height with no range check; root of the geometry integer issues.
Attacker-controlled, unbounded; drive both allocation and byte-count arithmetic.
Set only to fixed 128/32768 literals; used as element-width selector and seen-flag; no attacker value flows in. Note: not reset per iteration, but harmless once the bits[] break fires.
Signed destination of an unsigned overflow-prone product (line 49); can go negative or wrap.
Only h[0..3] ever written from input; h[2]/h[4] pre-NUL bound the sscanf string; no overflow of h itself.
Declared uninitialized and read at line 109 even when sscanf (line 107) performs no conversion — the CWE-908 core.
Correctly bounds line length and rejects over-long lines; a positive control, not a defect.
Bounded source; the risk is the unchecked value, tracked under the value/width/height anchors, not this call.
Unsigned overflow of attacker-controlled geometry stored into a signed int; classic size-arithmetic hazard.
Allocates from unchecked width*height — memory exhaustion and potential internal size overflow.
Return value discarded; on failure leaves b uninitialized — the trigger for the CWE-908 finding.
EOF checked at every read; writes stay within h[0..3]; risk is content (non-hex) not bounds — covered by the b finding.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: gdImageSetPixel(im, x++, y, ...) with x==im->sx / y==im->sy wrap (lines 109-117)
reviewed_anchors.reason: x resets at im->sx and y is checked against im->sy with early return; pixel writes stay in-canvas.
im is non-NULL here (checked at line 68) and freed exactly once; no NULL-deref or double-free.

findings.title: Use of uninitialized value b when hex parse fails (unchecked sscanf return)
findings.bug_class: Use of uninitialized resource (unchecked sscanf conversion, uninitialized read)
findings.invariant: b must be assigned a defined value before it is read; either initialize b = 0 or use it only when sscanf(h, "%x", &b) == 1.
findings.failure_mode: b is declared without an initializer (line 17). When the attacker places non-hex characters after an 'x' in the bits[] data, sscanf performs zero conversions and does not write b, yet line 109 reads b to compute every pixel of the element. The result is an uninitialized-value read: nondeterministic output, sanitizer/analyzer violations, and a potential info-leak of stale stack contents into pixel data.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm b has no earlier definite assignment on any path reaching line 109 and that sscanf's return value is never checked, so a crafted non-hex element yields an uninitialized read.

findings.title: Integer overflow in bitmap byte-count arithmetic
findings.invariant: width and height must be range-checked to a sane maximum, and the byte count computed in a type/order that cannot overflow, before it is used as a loop or buffer size.
findings.failure_mode: width and height are unbounded attacker values. (width + 7) wraps near UINT_MAX and the multiply overflows the 32-bit unsigned product; the value is then stored into the signed int bytes, which can become negative (loop body skipped, blank image) or a small wrong positive (data/canvas desync). Combined with the unchecked gdImageCreate allocation this yields inconsistent state and DoS.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no upper bound on width/height before line 49 and no overflow check on the product, and that bytes is a signed int receiving an unsigned result.

findings.title: Unbounded image allocation from unchecked width/height
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled resource / memory allocation (size not validated)
findings.invariant: width and height must be clamped to a documented maximum (and their product bounded) before being passed to gdImageCreate.
findings.failure_mode: Only width/height == 0 is filtered (via bytes). A file declaring huge dimensions (e.g. 60000x60000) makes gdImageCreate attempt multi-gigabyte allocation plus per-row pointer arrays, causing memory exhaustion / crash; on 32-bit the internal sx*sy size math can itself overflow into an undersized buffer.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no maximum-dimension check exists before gdImageCreate and inspect gdImageCreate's own size arithmetic for width*height overflow on the target ABI.

findings.title: Negative/oversized #define value cast to unsigned dimension
findings.anchor: width = (unsigned int) value; height = (unsigned int) value;
findings.invariant: Reject value <= 0 and value greater than a maximum image dimension before casting to unsigned.
findings.failure_mode: value is parsed with %d and may be negative; a '#define x_width -1' becomes width == 4294967295, arming the overflow (line 49) and the allocation (line 68). No lower or upper bound is enforced at the cast.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm value is used with no >0 / <=MAX guard between the sscanf at line 25 and the casts at lines 33/36.

findings.title: Width-less sscanf %s into iname (latent stack overflow)
findings.code:
if ( sscanf(fline, "static unsigned char %s = {", iname) == 1
			  || sscanf(fline, "static char %s = {", iname) == 1)
findings.bug_class: Unbounded format conversion into fixed buffer
findings.invariant: Every sscanf %s must carry an explicit field width no larger than the destination (e.g. %*s bounded to sizeof(iname)-1).
findings.failure_mode: The %s conversions targeting iname (lines 25, 39-40, 43-44) have no width limit. It is safe here only because iname and the fgets-bounded fline share MAX_XBM_LINE_SIZE and the line-length guard at line 22 caps input; if buffer sizes diverge or the format is reused on a longer source, a long token overflows iname on the stack. Flagged as a latent hardening issue.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm sizeof(iname) == sizeof(fline) and that line 22's guard is always active before these sscanf calls; add explicit field widths regardless.

audit_checklist: Confirm b is read at gdImageSetPixel (line 109) without a definite prior assignment when sscanf (line 107) fails on non-hex input — the CWE-908 core; fix by checking sscanf==1 or initializing b.
Verify there is NO upper bound on width/height before the byte-count arithmetic (line 49) and the gdImageCreate call (line 68).
Check (width + 7) / 8 * height for unsigned overflow and for a signed-int truncation when stored into bytes.
Confirm #define value is not validated (>0 and <= max) before the signed-to-unsigned casts at lines 33/36.
Inspect gdImageCreate's internal width*height size computation for overflow on 32-bit ABIs given attacker-chosen dimensions.
Confirm iname and fline are the same size and that all sscanf %s conversions lack field widths (lines 25, 39-40, 43-44).
